coredata.dc:title: Solvent-free growth of carbon dots by sputter-plasma assisted chemical vapour deposition over large areas
coredata.dc:creator: Park, Jaewoo
Bazylewski, Paul
Wong, Victor
Shah, Harshil
Fanchini, Giovanni
coredata.dc:description: Planar graphene quantum dots synthesized using wet chemistry have been widely investigated for their optoelectronic properties, but less work exists on deposition of curved carbon dots (CDs) by physical techniques. In this work, a bottom-up growth method termed sputter-plasma assisted chemical vapour deposition (SPA-CVD) is used to grow curved CDs in vacuum, reminiscent of graphene fabricated on copper foil using chemical vapour deposition. In SPA-CVD, ∼10-nm thick Cu layers with an ultrathin carbon film on top are nucleated into copper nanoparticles (Cu-NP) by thermal annealing resulting in curved CDs on their surfaces. CD morphology and dimensions are determined by the hemispherical shape of the nucleated Cu-NPs. With this method, a uniform layer of CDs of tuneable size can be grown on a substrate such as aluminum over wafer-size-areas, without the use of solvents. CD diameter and concentration can be tuned over a wide range by manipulating the SPA-CVD sputtering and evaporation parameters. It is shown that SPA-CVD grown curved CDs embedded in a poly(methyl methacrylate) matrix show resistive memory effects. This work provides a new insight towards the development of high-quality, curved CDs with tuneable properties for device applications, for which planar and solution processed CDs may not be suitable.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Transfer printing and resistive memory device fabrication and testing
Scanning/transmission electron microscopy and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy
Growth of carbon dots on Al foil by SPA-CVD
Transfer printing of carbon dot layers
Memory effects of transfer-printed carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Demonstration of CD fabrication using SPA-CVD: (a) deposition system with radio-frequency sputtering and thermal evaporation system in ultra-high vacuum. (b) Wafer-size Al foil is placed on a rotatable stage. (c) Cu is thermally evaporated onto the substrate to form a thin film. (d) C film is sputtered onto the Cu film to form a layered structure. (d) Annealing simultaneously transforms the Cu film into nanoparticles, and the C film into CDs on top of Cu-NPs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Diagram of transfer printing process used to demonstrate CD devices with resistive memory effects: (a) CDs fabricated on sacrificial Al foil by the process shown in Fig. 1 are b) coated with PMMA and dried (c) Al foil is etched by HCl to obtain freestanding CD/Cu-NP composites, followed by Cu etching in HNO3 to obtain CDs on PMMA. d) This PMMA film with CDs is transferred onto a pre-patterned ITO/glass substrate. (e) Interdigitated Al electrodes are thermally evaporated on top of CD/PMMA, resulting in sandwich Al/PMMA/CD/ITO devices. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

In-plane SEM images of CD/Cu-NPs formed on Al foils by sequential growth of Cu and C layers. The Cu layer was grown by thermal evaporation, while the C layer was grown on top of it by DC-biased RF sputtering in the same vacuum chamber. Subsequent in situ thermal annealing of the C/Cu bilayer resulted in the formation of Cu-NPs with CD on top. Results presented here are obtained from the following thicknesses (a) 1 nm Cu (no C), (b) 1 nm C/1 nm Cu, (c) 16 nm Cu/1 nm Cu, (d) 6 nm Cu (no C), (e) 1 nm C/6 nm Cu, and (f) 16 nm C/6 nm Cu. (g) Thickness of C film grown on top of Cu as a function of the sputter deposition time. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TEM of CDs at 52000× magnification showing a high density of CDs on the surface (b) Histogram showing the size range of CDs to be < 10 nm, with the highest percentage of CDs in the 9–10 nm range. (c) Raman spectroscopy of CD/Cu-NP composites substrate after annealing on a 1 nm Cu film with a varied carbon sputtering deposition time. (d) C 1s XPS spectrum of the CD/Cu-NP composites showing a prominent C-C peak which position is consistent with a curved and/or disordered structure of CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

SEM images after each step of transfer printing of CDs obtained from 1 nm C film on 1 nm Cu film. (a) PMMA transferred from bare Al and etched by HCl, (b) PMMA transferred from bare Al and etched by HCl and HNO3. (c) CD/Cu-NP composites formed from a 1 nm Cu film and 16 nm carbon layer on PMMA after removal of Al, (d) clusters of CDs on PMMA from panel c after further removal of Cu by HNO3 etching, and (e) uniformly distributed CDs on PMMA produced from a 1 nm Cu film and a 1 nm carbon film after the same etching process as in panel d.

(a) Contact-mode AFM image of CDs (grown by SPA-CVD from 1-nmthick Cu layer and 1-nm carbon film, annealed at 380 °C) on PMMA film. (b) Transmission SNOM, and (c) photo-luminescence SNOM. Coloured outlines indicate areas exhibiting the strongest PL. Comparison with panel b shows that while some of these areas are also the mostly optically absorbing, the correlation between high absorbance and high PL is not consistent because of the different curvature of CD aggregates in different regions. The inset on top of the figure demonstrates white-yellow PL of SPA-CVD CDs in solution, compared to a solution containing CD/Cu-NPs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Memory device architecture with a 500 nm thick PMMA film, and associated confocal optical images collected at different depths along the z-axis of the sample (0, 200 nm, and 400 nm). These optical transmission images show light scattering of off CD clusters in darker areas. b) SEM side view of the same memory device (1 nm C and 6 nm Cu) cut with FIB milling; (c) EDX images from the area shown in panel a, which indicate that the CDs are clustered near the ITO:PMMA interface and are still partially attached to Cu-NPs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Illustration of a Cu-NP/CD complex before etching of Cu, (b) After etching of Cu, the odd-numbered rings in the curved CDs possess SOMO levels that can be singly or doubly occupied, representing, ‘erase’ and ‘write’ cycles in a memory device, respectively. (c) I-V characteristics of these memory devices showing hysteresis effects. (d) Three current cycles resulting from the two alternating resistivity states in write-read-erase cycles at VW = 8 V to write, VR = 0.5 V to read and VE = −6 V to erase. (e) As average of 20 cycles of the same plot shown in d. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Solvent-free growth of carbon dots by sputter-plasma assisted chemical vapour deposition over large areas

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Astronomy, University of Western Ontario, 1151 Richmond St., London, ON, N6A 3K7, Canada

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Western Ontario, 1151 Richmond St., London, ON, N6A 3K7, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Planar graphene quantum dots synthesized using wet chemistry have been widely investigated for their optoelectronic properties, but less work exists on deposition of curved carbon dots (CDs) by physical techniques. In this work, a bottom-up growth method termed sputter-plasma assisted chemical vapour deposition (SPA-CVD) is used to grow curved CDs in vacuum, reminiscent of graphene fabricated on copper foil using chemical vapour deposition. In SPA-CVD, ∼10-nm thick Cu layers with an ultrathin carbon film on top are nucleated into copper nanoparticles (Cu-NP) by thermal annealing resulting in curved CDs on their surfaces. CD morphology and dimensions are determined by the hemispherical shape of the nucleated Cu-NPs. With this method, a uniform layer of CDs of tuneable size can be grown on a substrate such as aluminum over wafer-size-areas, without the use of solvents. CD diameter and concentration can be tuned over a wide range by manipulating the SPA-CVD sputtering and evaporation parameters. It is shown that SPA-CVD grown curved CDs embedded in a poly(methyl methacrylate) matrix show resistive memory effects. This work provides a new insight towards the development of high-quality, curved CDs with tuneable properties for device applications, for which planar and solution processed CDs may not be suitable.

ce:section.ce:para: Planar and crystalline graphene dots and carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively studied for their optical and electrical properties and are attractive for specific device applications due to their non-toxic nature and decreasing costs of fabrication. With these advantages, CDs are good candidates to complement and potentially replace inorganic materials used in electrical, optical, and biomedical device applications [1–3]. CDs need to meet the standard for industrialization, where the particular CD fabrication method plays an important role in determining the properties and total cost of the resultant CDs. Various techniques have been proposed to produce CDs efficiently while achieving desirable properties, with the fabrication processes introduced in the literature mainly categorised by two approaches; top down or bottom up fabrication [1,3].

CD top down fabrication processes utilize micro or nano-size flakes of graphene or other carbon-based materials that have been synthesized or otherwise obtained previously. These materials are reduced in size via a variety of chemical or physical techniques to achieve carbon nanoparticles suitable for a given application [4–15]. Bottom up approaches differ in that molecular sized precursors are used initially as components to construct CDs of a desired size [1,3]. Although chemical-based top-down or bottom-up methods are successful in producing CDs with a variety of properties, CDs fabricated by these methods continue to suffer from poor CD size control, aggregation [3–9,13], and limited scalability for mass production [12]. Moreover, additional steps are often required to modify the electrical and optical properties of CDs once they have been produced to suit a given application [1,3]. These disadvantages can be overcome through the use of physical fabrication methods that do not rely on solution-based processing.

In this work, bottom-up fabrication of CDs is reported using a sputter-plasma assisted chemical vapour deposition (SPA-CVD) process. This method has the potential to produce large amounts of CDs in a short time. CDs are formed from a sputter-plasma grown carbon film on top of a layer of copper nanoparticles that act as a catalyst for the carbon phase nucleation. Annealing this structure in high vacuum results in a layer of Cu nanoparticles (Cu-NPs) covered by curved CDs of tuneable diameter, depending on the Cu-NP diameter and deposition parameters.

A decisive advantage of SPA-CVD is that it is carried out in ultra-high vacuum (UHV), which minimizes the presence of impurities during the process. Carrying out the copper catalyst deposition and carbon growth in the same UHV chamber, with the sample stage that can be rotated face-down for thermal evaporation of Cu and face-up for plasma-assisted growth of CDs further minimizes the effects of contamination. Considering the high deposition uniformity and substrate flexibility achievable with sputter-plasma processes, SPA-CVD is able to deposit CDs over wafer size areas and on a variety of different rigid or flexible substrates [16]. Contrary to conventional wet-chemistry methods used for CD growth, another significant advantage of SPA-CVD is the CD edges do not contain any functional groups introduced by the fabrication, which enables in situ or post-deposition functionalization. Hence SPA-CVD is a versatile bottom-up technique with significant advantages over wet chemistry techniques for CD growth.

CDs fabricated in this work have been characterised by electron microscopies, Raman and photoemission spectroscopies and photoluminescence to investigate their size, morphology, crystallinity and optoelectronic properties. The solvent-free deposition process produces a uniform layer of CDs that is ideal for applications requiring CDs closely packed in thin film layers. Another important aspect to be considered is that CDs growth methods developed so far normally lead to planar and highly luminescent graphene quantum dots [1–3]. Use of hemispherical Cu-NPs in SPA-CVD may lead to curved, and more disordered, CDs. These are anticipated to be less luminescent than planar graphene dots due to the presence of non-radiative recombination centres relating to de presence of fivefold rings in curved carbon surfaces [17,18]. On the other hand, these centres are potentially charge-trapping and may lead to resistive memory effects if the CDs are incorporated throughout or at the interface of an insulating thin film. Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) can be used to transfer a CD layer onto a conducting electrode as the basis for a non-volatile resistive memory device. Memristor type devices produced from SPA-CVD deposited CDs are demonstrated, with read-write-erase cycles that can be used to store data at relatively low voltages.

The SPA-CVD process used for CD fabrication is illustrated in Fig. 1 a. CDs were prepared using a wafer size aluminium foil as a substrate (Alcan Standard 100’, Fig. 1b). Other substrates (Si and BK7 glass) were also used for additional characterization. All substrates were positioned on a rotatable stage inside the deposition system, consisting in a cylindrical vacuum chamber (10−7 Torr base pressure) equipped with a 13.56 MHz radio-frequency (RF) sputtering system above the stage and a thermal evaporation device (K.J. Lesker Inc.) below. The evaporator was powered by a Hewlett-Packard 6466C DC power supply. A DC voltage from a Canberra 3010 power supply connected to the stage can be used to bias the RF sputtering system. The stage is electrically insulated from the chamber and is equipped with a heater for sample annealing. The heater is connected to the grid through an insulation transformer. Radio-frequency can be applied to the sputtering gun using a RF-3 generator (RF VII Inc.) and PT-II-CE matching network. A 2” diameter, 99.99% purity, graphite target is mounted on the RF sputtering head.

Layers of copper with thickness adjusted from 1 nm to 10 nm were thermally evaporated (7.5 V and 65 A) from tungsten basket heaters loaded with Cu pellets (99.99% purity) and placed in alumina crucibles (EVA9AO, K.J. Lesker Inc.) as shown in Fig. 1c. Cu layer thickness was measured in situ using a quartz oscillator connected to a Sycon STM-2 monitor. The rotatable stage was subsequently tilted upwards and carbon (1–16 nm thickness) was sputtered at 80 W RF power and 0.5 nm/min deposition rate on top of the Cu layers (Fig. 1d). A −100 V DC stage bias was used during RF sputter deposition. Argon (30 sccm flow rate, determined by an Omega FMA 5400 flow metre) was used as a sputter gas. The sample was then annealed at 380 °C for 1 h in situ on the stage to nucleate the Cu and C films into Cu-NPs and CDs, respectively (Fig. 1e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transfer printing and resistive memory device fabrication and testing
ce:section.ce:para: Devices were fabricated by removing the CD layers from sacrificial Al foils using a PMMA-based transfer printing method demonstrated in Fig. 2 . 80 mg of PMMA powder (Goodfellow Cambridge Ltd, LS311758) was dissolved in chlorobenzene at 8% wt ratio prior to transfer printing. This PMMA solution was spin coated on top of the CD/Cu-NP composite at 1000 rpm for 1 min followed by drying at 3000 rpm for 3 min. The aluminium substrate was then etched in concentrated HCl (Caledon, CAS 7647-01-0) for 10 min, followed by 3 baths in deionized water for 10 min each. Samples, consisting of PMMA layers with CDs and Cu-NPs at their surface, were subsequently etched in concentrated HNO3 (Caledon, CAS 7697-37-2) to remove the Cu-NPs, followed by 3 water baths as above. This resulted in a layer of CDs on the bottom of the PMMA film as shown in Fig. 2c.

To assemble resistive memory devices, PMMA/Cu-NP/CD layers were transferred onto prepatterned ITO substrates (Sigma-Aldrich, 75 Ω/□) as shown in Fig. 2d and dried at 50 °C for 10’. This was followed by the deposition of 200 nm-thick Al interdigitated electrodes on top of PMMA layer, as shown in Fig. 2e to form sandwich structures along with the bottom ITO cathodes. In this way, 16 independent “0/1” memory devices could be fabricated onto the same chip, and statistics on their performance could be obtained. Resistive memory device characterization was carried out both by recording current-voltage (I-V) cycles (from 0 to + V, then down to – V, and vice versa) and by performing, on each device, up to 100 read-write-erase-read cycles. Read, write and erase levels were optimized at VR = 0.5 V, VW = +8 V and VE = −6 V respectively. Both measurement types were recorded in a probe station (Signatone S-725-CLM probes) placed under nitrogen atmosphere (Nexus II glovebox, Vacuum Atmosphere Inc.) by using a Keithley 2400 source meter programmed by a Matlab™ routine. Data reported here are representative of 16 interdigitated “1/0” devices on the same chip, produced from annealed 6-nm Cu and 1-nm C bilayers. Fabrication of each chip has been repeated up to five times to ensure device reproducibility. Optimization of CD growth parameters and PMMA thickness for optimal memory device performance is beyond the scope of this paper and will be reported in future work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning/transmission electron microscopy and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurements were performed using a Zeiss LEO 1540XD SEM system. The surface of all samples was coated with 5 nm osmium to increase conductivity using a Filgen OPC80T Os coater prior to measurement. Samples were attached to a flat aluminium stub using conducting carbon tape. Silver paint (Pelco® colloidal Ag) was applied on the side of the samples to improve conductivity between the samples and aluminium stub. SEM images were measured by detecting secondary electrons at 1 kV. Cross-sectional SEM experiments have been carried out on some of the resistive memory devices described in section 2.2 by cutting the sample using a focused ion beam (FIB) utilizing Ga+ ions at 30 kV energy and 40 pA current attached to the SEM system. The sample cross-section was subsequently imaged by SEM, and analysed using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) for elemental analysis. Confocal optical images of the devices were also acquired, using a Witec 300 confocal optical microscope using a 532 nm laser to illuminate the sample.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out using a Philips 420 TEM at an accelerating voltage of 60 kV and 54,000× magnification, with the CD samples mounted on Ni grids coated with a 1–2 nm layer of holey carbon. The CD sample was prepared by dissolving a CD/Cu-NP sample on Al foil in a mixture of HCl and HNO3 to remove the Cu-NPs and the aluminium substrate. The resulting acid-CD solution was drop-cast onto the TEM grid and washed with water before measurements. TEM images were analysed to determine the particle size distribution using the ImageJ software [19]. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) measurements on CD/Cu-NP on Al foil were carried out using a Kratos AXIS Ultra spectrometer and a Al Kα X-ray source at 1486.6 eV energy.

Unpolarised Raman spectra were recorded in the 100-3200 cm−1 spectral region on a Renishaw InVia spectrometer equipped with a Leica DM2600 M microscope and a 20× microscope objective in a backscattering arrangement. A 633 nm helium-neon laser with a maximum output power of 17 mW was used as the excitation source. The power at the sample surface was 0.085 mW/μm2 across a 20 μm2 area. Special attention was paid to avoid damage of the sample by the laser by shuttering the laser when not actively measuring. Raman spectra were recorded using a charge-coupled device (CCD) array of 1024 × 256 pixels, which was cooled to −70 °C using a Peltier cell.

Photoluminescence (PL) images of CDs were recorded at 405 nm excitation using a Witec Alpha 300 S aperture-type scanning near-field optical microscopy (SNOM). SNOM [20] is a nano-optical scanning probe technique with the potential to simultaneously obtain atomic force microscopy (AFM) and optical images with nanoscale resolution beyond the diffraction limit. This is possible because the sample is illuminated (or light is collected) through a nano-sized aperture in the AFM tip, much smaller than a wavelength, which permits SNOM to generate (or detect) evanescent near-field radiation localized a few nm in the proximity of a sample [20]. PL and other spectroscopic and imaging techniques can be implemented on a SNOM microscope, which is extremely advantageous when individual nanostructures, such as CD, need to be investigated. In these SNOM-PL experiments, a contact-mode SNOM cantilever (SNOM-C, NT-MDT Co.) with a 120 ± 25 nm aperture opened in an AFM the tip was used. Images were recorded in collection mode, with the laser exciting the samples from below, using an inverted optical microscope. The sample, deposited on transparent BK7 glass substrate, is placed on an x-y piezo-scanner. Emitted photons were collected in the evanescent near-field through the aperture of the SNOM tip. Rayleigh light is rejected from the PL images using a 405 nm notch filter (NF405-13, Thorlabs, Inc.) mounted between the SNOM tip and the photomultiplier detector of the instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth of carbon dots on Al foil by SPA-CVD
ce:section.ce:para: Cu is frequently used in chemical vapour deposition (CVD) of graphene due to its weak bonding to carbon and because graphene domains grown on flat Cu surfaces have no rotational alignment with the Cu lattice when polycrystalline Cu is used as a support [21–23]. Large and crystalline graphene domains are produced by CVD regardless of the nature of Cu. In our SPA-CVD method, we extend copper-based CVD growth of graphene to the growth of curved CDs, by using curved Cu-NPs as supports in lieu of a flat and polycrystalline Cu sheet. It has been previously shown that hemispherical Cu-NPs with tuneable diameter and concentration can be reproducibly grown by annealing thin layers of thermally evaporated Cu at relatively low temperatures (200oC-500 °C, comparable to 380 °C used in this work) [24]. The diameter and concentration of Cu-NPs depends primarily on the thickness of the initial Cu film, along with the temperature and duration of the annealing process. It is worth noting that Al foils are always passivated by a thin and uniform layer of alumina, and therefore Cu-NP nucleation occurs on aluminium oxide. Deposition of a thicker Cu film increases the diameter of resulting Cu-NPs, while high temperature and longer annealing times decreases their diameter and concentration [25]. SPA-CVD uses the tunability of Cu-NP diameter and concentration to control the morphology of CDs that form when the carbon layer corrugates on top of Cu-NPs due to the nucleation of the Cu phase. This effect is facilitated by the tendency of sputter and plasma-based techniques to form smooth and uniform ultrathin amorphous carbon films on top of a variety of different substrates [26].

Fig. 3 shows that the quality of SPA-CVD grown CDs is affected by the thickness of the pristine carbon films. While an ultrathin C film (Fig. 3b) minimally affects the nucleation of the underlying Cu phase, excessive C deposition (Fig. 3c) may result both in an excess of amorphous carbon around CDs that might still be forming at the Cu interface, and in a higher stiffness of the C/Cu bilayer film. Greater stiffness may lead to a diminished nucleation rate of Cu-NPs and ultimately smaller CDs. While the two effects are difficult to be decoupled, SEM images appear to indicate that the first effect is dominant for thin Cu layers (Fig. 3 a–c) where the diameter of Cu-NPs appear to be the same even under a thick C layer (panel c). Conversely, the second effect is evident from thicker Cu layers in Fig. 3d–f, where depositing a thicker C film (panel f) seems to result in smaller CD/Cu-NPs. This is a result of the Cu-NP formation process where metal nanoparticles are formed to minimize the interface energy between Cu and vacuum [27]. When a relatively thick layer of carbon is deposited on top of Cu, the C/Cu interface energy is lower than the Cu/vacuum interface energy. This causes the CD/Cu-NP composites to nucleate much slower, requiring annealing times greater than 1 h to form. Nevertheless, the images in Fig. 3 indicate that CDs can be produced with a controlled size and density by precisely controlling the thickness of both Cu and C layers.

A typical TEM image of CDs, grown using 6 nm Cu and 1 nm C, is shown in Fig. 4 a after removal of Cu-NPs. A high concentration of CDs is evident, which is indicative of the advantages of the SPA-CVD growth technique. A CD diameter histogram, presented in Fig. 4b, shows a bimodal distribution in the CD diameters. Nearly 40% of the CDs have a narrow diameter distribution, in the 8–10 nm range, which is consistent with the Cu-NP diameter. When analysed by TEM, the remaining CDs appear to have a significantly smaller diameter, down to 3–5 nm and typically possess a higher aspect ratio. This may occur because our CDs are grown on hemispherical Cu-NPs with a curved shape, causing some of the CDs to tilt on the TEM grid, showing their lateral side. This may explain why the apparent size distribution of CDs produced by SPA-CVD departs from a monomodal distribution, generally observed for CDs fabricated using wet-chemistry [14,15]. By contrast, SPA-CVD produces CDs with a fixed maximum size excluding aggregates, which is dependent on the thickness of Cu and C films deposited. Aside from CDs possessing the maximum size, all other CDs are observed in repeated cases to be smaller in size, as shown in Fig. 4b. This property makes the size distribution of CDs produced using SPA-CVD highly controllable over a narrow range without additional treatment, which is ideal for device applications.

The vibrational properties of CD/Cu-NPs were investigated using Raman spectroscopy at 633 nm excitation (Fig. 4c). sp2 carbon materials exhibit two distinct Raman-active vibrations, the G and D-peaks [27]. The G-peak is centred at 1581 cm−1, while the D-peak centre decreases at increasing excitation wavelength and is centred around 1350 cm−1 at 633 nm. The D/G peak intensity ratio is a measure of the nonideality of sp2 bonded carbon domains, including their finite size, amorphization, curvature and point defect concentration [28,29]. Spectra in Fig. 4c show that no G and D-peaks are detectable from Cu-coated Al foil and up to 2 min C sputtering. Although 633 nm excitation was selected to optimize the Raman peak intensity over PL, a relatively strong PL background still shadows the Raman spectra. D and G-peaks become visible after 4 min C sputtering, corresponding to a 2 nm thick C layer. Both Raman peaks are significantly broader than their graphene counterparts. The presence of the D peak is consistent with disorder due to curvature, and/or finite carbon domains. The relatively high D/G ratio and broad D and G peaks show that the CDs are partly disordered in nature, which is expected because the crystal structure of carbon on Cu is highly dependent on the temperature during CD growth and rearrangement, which in this case is 380 °C, where 800–1000 °C is required to produce planar graphene domains by CVD [21–23].

An XPS spectrum of CD/Cu-NPs on Al foil is shown in Fig. 4d and can be used to gain further insight into the structure of the CDs. The C 1s spectrum contains four components corresponding to C=C bonds (284.8 eV); C-C or C-O bonds (both about 286 eV); C=O bonds (287.8 eV); and O-C=O bonds (288.8 eV) [30]. The most prominent of these features is associated to C=C sp2 carbon bonding, with a significant C-C feature as well, in the proportions normally observed in fullerenes and other forms of curved carbons [30,31]. The remaining peaks indicate oxygen functional groups present due to atmospheric exposure of the CDs. To summarize, characterization of the CDs indicates that a significant proportion of sp2 C=C bonds are formed in our process, with some additional C-C bonds in a proportion consistent with curved CDs, and in agreement with the curved shape of Cu-NPs involved in their fabrication process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transfer printing of carbon dot layers
ce:section.ce:para: Transfer printing is a general method used to move target materials to a desired substrate using a flexible thin film as a carrier. The flexible film can be coated over the target material using spin-coating or spray-coating. This thin film coated sample may then undergo an etching process to remove undesired materials, while leaving the desired materials untouched. The transfer printing process used in this study is shown in Fig. 2, where a PMMA film is used as the thin film carrier for CD/Cu-NP composites.

CDs were transferred onto ITO and glass substrates, with SEM investigations performed at each step as shown in Fig. 5 . Panels a and b show the bottom surfaces of a PMMA film transferred after spin coating onto an Al substrate without any CDs or Cu-NPs after HCl etching (panel a), or a combination of HCl and HNO3 etching (panel b). Stripes printed from the morphology of the bare Al foil can be clearly seen in Fig. 5a. These stripes are the consequence of micro-trenches in Al foil introduced in the casting process, which are also evident in Fig. 3a–c and show that CD/Cu-NP composites are grown thin enough on Al to still see the stripes of the substrate. These stripes become barely visible due to slight damage after the nitric acid etching shown in Fig. 5b. However, the morphology of the PMMA differs in samples with CD/Cu-NP composites present on the surface before etching. Fig. 5c shows CD/Cu-NP composites on PMMA after HCl exposure from a sample originally containing a 1 nm Cu film and 16 nm carbon film. Larger particles are visible on the surface after etching, with a size indicating they are CD/Cu-NP aggregates that are not present as a uniform layer. Small holes are also visible in the PMMA surface, indicating positions where CD/Cu-NP composites were originally embedded in the film. Gas evolution during the aluminum etching process is vigorous enough to dislodge the CD/Cu-NPs embedded at the surface of the PMMA film, leaving small holes that are visible by SEM. The CD/Cu-NP clusters change in morphology after the nitric acid as shown in Fig. 5d, where the surface of the CD/Cu-Np aggregates has been smoothed, and the holes in the PMMA are eliminated. Although PMMA is resistant to chemical damage from nitric acid, local heating of the surface occurs when Cu is etched that causes deformation of the PMMA surface. The deformation ultimately acts to smooth the PMMA surface and close the holes seen in Fig. 5c. These images illustrate that larger CDs are not easily transfer printed and etching in this case produces micron size carbon aggregates in the final product rather than CDs.

Smaller CDs can be transferred to glass as a homogeneous layer, as shown in Fig. 5e. When a thin carbon layer is used, a high-density, uniform layer of particles is obtained after HCl and HNO3 etching. This result indicates that smaller CDs are more easily transferrable via this transfer printing method combined with etching. A possible explanation for the effect that prevents larger CDs from being successfully transferred is due to the CD surface area/volume ratio. Larger CDs or CD/Cu-NP composites have relatively low surface potential compared to smaller CDs or CD/Cu-NP composites [25]. Therefore, smaller CDs or CD/Cu-NP composites are more easily enveloped by the PMMA film, whereas the larger particles have a smaller surface area of contact and are more easily detached from the film during transfer or etching. The clusters appear only after etching of the Al substrate, also implying that the vigorous etching is likely to be the cause of cluster formation. Significant local heating during the Al etching causes partial deformation of PMMA film, while gas evolution may create enough turbulence for Cu-NPs to be physically detached from the PMMA film. This separation of Al substrate from PMMA film prevents the successful transfer of CD/Cu-NPs, but can be avoided by focusing on smaller CDs and Cu-NPs that can be transferred intact for use in device applications.

Luminescence is an important property of planar graphene dots grown by wet-chemistry, which enables them for several applications in eco-friendly optoelectronic devices [1–3]. As far as curved carbon nanostructures are concerned, their PL yield is known to be significantly lower than in planar nanostructures due to the presence of fivefold and/or sevenfold aromatic rings [17,18]. Odd-numbered rings are associated to singly-occupied molecular orbitals (SOMOs) in the proximity of the Fermi level, which act as non-radiative recombination centres. The top inset of Fig. 6 compares the luminescence from a water-based solution of CDs with a solution containing only Cu-NPs. The CD solution exhibits white-yellow PL, which qualitatively that the CDs display luminescence, even in the presence of disorder and curvature.

Correlation between nanostructure formation and luminescence can be investigated using SNOM-PL imaging at the nanoscale. Fig. 6a is an AFM image of SPA-CVD CDs grown on Al foil, and transferred on PMMA. Clusters of CDs at different degrees of curvature can be observed where the removed Al foil was corrugated. Fig. 6b is a transmission SNOM image of the same region as in panel a, where the darker areas correspond to lower light absorption, due to higher CD concentration. If PL was homogeneously generated by CDs, the darker regions in panel b would always correspond to brighter regions in Fig. 6c that is the corresponding PL image. Comparison of the regions highlighted by coloured dotted lines correspond to the areas with the highest PL and show this is not the case. For example, the area highlighted in red offers relatively low PL as seen in panel c, while also showing significantly higher absorbance than the area highlighted in green (panel b), and corresponding to CDs with higher curvature as can be seen in panel a. Although a direct correlation between lower curvature and higher PL is difficult to establish, this is a strong indication that curvature may play a role in CD PL yield. In any case, the relatively low PL of SPA-CVD produced curved CDs seems to be a consequence of their specific growth process utilizing curved Cu-NPs as templates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Memory effects of transfer-printed carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: While the relatively weak PL of curved CDs suggests they are not ideal for use in light-emitting devices, the presence of SOMO gap states acting as non-radiative PL recombination centres enables their use in other applications. Thin film memory devices produced from organic materials consisting of thin films with multi-stable resistivity characteristics are attractive as possible substitutes for inorganic memory devices due to lower fabrication costs [32–34]. Silicon-based flash devices currently dominate the market of non-volatile memory devices with multiple read-write-erase cycles, but suffer from high fabrication costs and minimal room for further performance improvement [35]. CDs produced by SPA-CVD are ideal for this application, as they can be assembled and transfer-printed in high-density layers.

Memory effects in SPA-CVD CDs have been investigated by fabricating resistive memory devices in sandwich configuration, as shown schematically in Fig. 7 a. With this configuration, the same PMMA layer used for transfer-printing is utilized as the insulating matrix in which CDs are embedded. The advantage of this method is a device fabrication process requiring one less step. A potential disadvantage is in the relatively low uniformity of the active layer, as shown by the transmission confocal optical images reported in Fig. 7a. Darker areas indicate light scattering from clusters of CDs, which can be seen identically in all of the depth slices due to the thinness of the PMMA layer.

However, the CD distribution in the PMMA layer is not homogeneous along the z-axis. Fig. 7b shows cross-sectional SEM image of a CD/PMMA based memory device. The image shows a gradient of CDs (some of them still encapsulating a Cu-NP) with their concentration decreasing away from the ITO/glass substrate. However this is not disadvantageous as it had also been previously shown that uniform nanostructure distribution along the z-axis is often not the optimal architecture for resistive memory devices, as they may benefit from the addition of an insulating layer in the proximity of the Al anode [36]. Therefore, it is expected that a nonuniform CD distribution will not prevent the observation of memory effects in our study.

Elemental mapping in the cross-section outlined by the red box is shown in Fig. 7c. The presence of a Cu EDX signal indicates that Cu-NPs are not completely removed by the etching process carried out during transfer printing. Comparison of Fig. 7b and c also corroborates that CDs are more concentrated at the boundary between PMMA and ITO, while their concentration in PMMA decreases moving away from the interface. Control experiments on Cu-NPs in PMMA and/or CD/Cu-NPs in PMMA without Cu-NP etching indicate insignificant memory effects, suggesting the observed memory effects should be assigned to Cu-free CDs.

CDs observed in the cross-sectional SEM (Fig. 7b) appear to be curved in shape, supporting the presence of curved sp2 carbon on the surface of spherical Cu-NPs. Curved sp2 structures present in the CDs may be neutral such as cyclopentadiene or negatively charged in the case of a cyclopentadienyl anion [30], as depicted in Fig. 8 a–b. Theses curved structures with a switchable charge state can provide the charge trapping effects that are necessary for a memory device to function. If the electron energy levels of the nanoparticles can be modified when the nanoparticles are charged or discharged, curved SPA-CVD-grown CDs may be ideal for applications in volatile and non-volatile resistive memory devices [33,34,36–39].

A typical I-V characteristic of one of these devices with CDs obtained from a carbon layer with 1 nm thickness on top 1 nm Cu film (see Figs. 3b and 5e), is shown in Fig. 8c. From this figure, the I-V curve is clearly non-ohmic [40,41] due to the presence of CDs containing curved carbon structures embedded in the insulating PMMA matrix. Fig. 8a and b shows schematically how curved CDs can become charged when they possess odd-numbered rings with SOMOs in the proximity of the Fermi level. The electron occupation of the SOMO levels then determines the overall charge state of the CDs, and this mechanism can be used to store charge and information.

The I-V curve in Fig. 8c describes the charging and discharging of CDs, which can be interpreted as ‘writing’ and ‘erasing’ data in the case of a memory device. If a positive write voltage (VW) > 3 V is reached through an initial direct I-V scan (grey trace in panel c), the CDs become charged, filling the available low-energy states in the CD potential well. This increase of potential energy leads to an increase in resistivity of the CDs, and less current flow through the device. The shape of the I-V curve changes when the scanning voltage is swept to from positive to negative polarity (black trace), becoming less steep at V ≈ 0, until reaching a negative ‘erase’ voltage (VE) < −3 V. At this point, the electrons in CDs are discharged, erasing the information stored in the previous write stage. A subsequent direct I-V scan up to VW > 3 V brings overlaps well (red trace) with the first direct scan (grey trace).

Fig. 8d and e shows the read-write performance of a CD/PMMA memory device over multiple write-erase cycles. In this measurement, a range of voltages were applied to charge and discharge the CD/PMMA layer. VW = 8 V was applied to charge the CDs and write to the device, while VE = −6 V was applied for 10 s to discharge and erase the device. After every VW and VE, a read voltage VR = 0.5 V was applied for 10 s to measure the current without changing the charge state of the device. Three cycles of VE, VR, VW and VR on a CD/PMMA memory device are recorded in Fig. 8d, showing the current at VR for each read-write-erase state of the CDs (the upper plot with white background) after each applied electric potential step (shown in the lower plot with grey background). The difference in current when reading the device in either low or high resistivity state can be clearly seen in the figure. Applying VE brings the memory device to the lower resistivity erase state with a current of 0.042 ± 0.001 mA, whereas writing to the device with VW brings the device to a higher resistivity state with write current of 0.036 ± 0.001 mA. The difference between the two states at VR = 0.5 V is approximately 6.0 ± 0.5 μA. The currents in both conductivity states are reproducible and highly stable, resulting from different local electron population in the CDs created by the charge and discharge potentials, VW and VE. The difference between the two states can be distinguished without destroying the current state of the device by applying a relatively low VR.

These results show that CDs produced by SPA-CVD can be used in thin film device applications with minimal post-processing and may be attractive certain applications such as memory devices where CDs produced by other methods are not suitable. Compared to silicon-based flash memory devices consisting of a metal–oxide–semiconductor field effect transistors, CD/PMMA based devices offer a lower potential cost and tuneable properties afforded by the size of the CDs. CD based devices have potential to compete with silicon-based memory devices which suffer from high fabrication costs, as well as minimum thickness limitation of 32 nm [42]. Organic memory devices based on CDs have the potential to surpass this thickness limit at a lower fabrication cost.

In conclusion, CDs have been fabricated by SPA-CVD on a wafer size Al substrate. SPA-CVD uses a carbon film deposited by sputtering on top if a Cu film on an Al substrate, which is annealed to transform the layered structure into a layer of CD/Cu-NP composites. The structure and size of the CDs can be influenced by the annealing duration and thickness of the carbon and Cu films. Although changing the annealing temperature was not investigated here, changes to the nucleation of Cu-NPs at different annealing temperatures is expected to affect the resulting size of CDs, where larger Cu-NPs produce larger CDs. Thicker Cu films combined with a thinner carbon film result in larger CDs, while keeping both layers thin with a longer annealing duration decreases the CD size. Although this study was performed using an Al substrate, SPA-CVD can be used to grow CDs on any type of substrate over large areas that can withstand the annealing temperature required. The application of SPA-CVD CDs in a memory device is demonstrated, showing resistive memory effects at low voltages using CDs embedded in an insulting PMMA layer. The unique curved structure of CDs presented in this study contains odd-numbered carbon rings that may be charged of discharged to provide the write and erase states of a memristor when the CDs are embedded in an insulating matrix.

A significant advantage of SPA-CVD CDs comes from their applicability to a wide range of applications. CDs prepared by this method are advantageous for some applications as they are prepared in a single homogeneous layer, with a maximum CD size that can be finely controlled by the fabrication parameters. Unlike many types of CDs produced by wet methods, SPA-CVD CDs do not possess any functional groups attached during the fabrication process, which can simplify their later use in devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: PB acknowledges an Ontario Centres of Excellence Talent Edge Postdoctoral Fellowship GF acknowledges a Canada Research Chair in Carbon-based nanomaterials and Nano-optoelectronics. Funding from the Canada Foundation for Innovation (award no. 212442) and the Discovery Grant program of the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (RGPIN-2015-06004) are also gratefully acknowledged. We gratefully acknowledge that part of this work was completed at the Western University Nanofabrication Facility (WUNF), the Biotron, and Surface Science Western (SSW). We would like to thank Dr. Mark Biesinger at SSW for his help with XPS, Mr. Stephen Hearn for TEM support, and Dr. Todd Simpson at WUNF for his assistance with the cross-sectional FIB-SEM measurements on CD resistive memory devices.
